summary:
Trade between the [[event:46|Indus Valley Civilisation]] and Mesopotamia was active approximately 2600–2000 BC and represents one of the most extensive long-distance commercial networks of the Bronze Age. Mesopotamian cuneiform records from this period, particularly during the Akkadian Empire and the Third Dynasty of Ur, mention a trading partner called Meluhha, which the majority of scholars identify as the Indus Valley region. Goods described as coming from Meluhha include copper, gold, carnelian beads, exotic woods, and possibly cotton textiles and livestock.

The trade was likely conducted via two main routes: an overland route through the Iranian plateau and a maritime route through the Persian Gulf, with the intermediate trading ports of [[event:1747|Dilmun (Bahrain)]] and [[event:1748|Magan (Oman)]] serving as entrepôts. Indus-style seals have been discovered at Mesopotamian sites including Ur, while Mesopotamian cylinder seals and administrative artefacts have been found in Indus contexts, confirming two-way contact. The port city of [[event:1744|Lothal]] in Gujarat was probably a major point of embarkation for maritime trade.

This trade network had significant consequences for both ends. It connected the Indus cities to the urban markets of Mesopotamia and provided a channel through which luxury goods, technical knowledge, and possibly ideas could flow across vast distances. The volume and regularity of this exchange contributed to the prosperity of both civilisations during their respective peaks. When the Indus Civilisation declined around 1900 BC, the references to Meluhha in Mesopotamian records fade, suggesting that the trade networks and the civilisation that sustained them collapsed together.
